data.title: HHIC:CA: High Income Exposure To Canadian Equities And Commodity Linkages
data.description:
Investment Case

Harvest Canadian High Income Shares ETF - Class A (HHIC:CA) currently offers a distribution yield of around 14%, largely attributable to the incremental returns generated by the ETF rather than distributions from NAVs in the form of return of capital. The fund has a commodity-heavy exposure profile owing to its focus on Canadian equities, a market structurally tilted toward energy, mining, and resource businesses.

The option overlay further enhances the income profile of the fund. Unlike many single-stock option income ETFs, HHIC writes options partially and at the portfolio level, rather than fully covering individual holdings. As a result, a major limitation of covered-call strategies of severely capped upside is mitigated to a meaningful extent, though not entirely eliminated.

HHIC also stands to benefit if the commodity cycle remains favorable or if energy and resource prices experience sharp upward movements. The value-oriented characteristics of many underlying holdings further enhance the attractiveness of the fund for value-focused investors. In this structure, regular distributions combined with potential NAV appreciation tend to benefit income-seeking investors in a dual manner.

However, the potential risks shall be kept in mind as well. The same value-oriented and commodity-linked companies may underperform materially if the commodity cycle reverses, which could lead to distribution reductions or NAV erosion. Nevertheless, on a risk-adjusted basis, the fund may still appeal to high-income-seeking investors who are willing to absorb the risk factors inherent in the strategy.

Introduction

Harvest Canadian High Income Shares ETF ("HHIC:CA") is a recently launched fund that seeks to provide unitholders with monthly cash distributions along with the opportunity for capital appreciation by investing on a leveraged basis.

The fund may hold direct positions in a portfolio of Canadian-listed equities or, alternatively, invest in exchange-traded funds that provide exposure to single Canadian equities.

As of now, the fund holds positions in the following entities.

Canadian equity markets are commodity heavy and generally exhibit a value tilt. This makes such securities attractive for investors with a constructive view on commodity cycles or resource-linked equities. This assertion is supported by the portfolio snapshot above as well. Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM), Cameco Corporation (CCJ), Canadian Natural Resources Limited (CNQ), and Suncor Energy Inc. (SU) collectively account for 50% of the portfolio allocation. The remaining exposure consists largely of banks and utilities, while Shopify Inc. (SHOP) remains the only technology or growth-oriented company in the portfolio. Also note that the fund employs leverage, which means the total exposure may generally range around 120–130% of net asset value at a given time due to the effect of leverage.

What I particularly like about the option overlay strategy employed by this fund, as opposed to other single-stock option income funds, is that the fund writes options on only up to 50% of the portfolio exposure. For that matter, the option writing level for February stayed around 33%. While this does cap the upside on the portion where calls are written, it does not entirely eliminate upside participation, as a meaningful part of the portfolio remains uncovered. In addition, since the fund holds a basket of securities, even if a particular holding were to be partially called away due to the option overlay, the impact at the overall fund level would not be very drastic.

The latest distribution was $0.18 per unit, which translates to a monthly pre-tax yield of roughly 1.25% at the current market price of $14.39. However, the distribution prior to that amounted to $0.16 per month. As a working estimate, the annualized distribution yield may therefore be considered in the range of roughly 13–14%. It is also worth noting that a significant portion of the distribution is typically characterized as capital gains and return of capital rather than ordinary dividends. As a result, the after-tax yield for investors remains more favorable, depending on their individual tax circumstances, however.

The ETF accompanies a leverage of around 25%; therefore, the fund may be particularly suitable for investors with a higher risk appetite, a constructive view on commodities and resources, and a focus on generating regular distributions. Nevertheless, the use of leverage exposes the fund to amplified downside risk during adverse market movements. As a reason, the strategy remains more suitable for investors with an ability to withstand occasional sharp drawdowns.

However, since the fund holds a basket of securities, even the downside impact arising from leverage is partly moderated by diversification across holdings. Therefore, to me, even this leveraged structure appears more balanced than an unleveraged exposure to a similar option strategy in a single-stock ETF.

Performance Evaluation

Since HHIC was launched only in August 2025, the available performance history is still relatively limited. Nevertheless, it is still somewhat useful to see how the fund has behaved relative to broader indices since inception.

As the chart indicates, HHIC has outperformed both the S&P/TSX Composite Index and the S&P 500 by a meaningful margin since inception. The idiosyncratic alpha generated by the ETF, along with regular distributions, does not appear insignificant. While the performance period is still too short to draw definitive conclusions, it is nevertheless reasonable to expect the strategy to perform relatively well during commodity upcycles, given the portfolio’s heavy exposure to resource-linked equities.

Further, the price return of roughly 18.47% suggests that a meaningful portion of the total return has come from appreciation in the fund’s net asset value, which reflects positively on the sustainability and quality of the distributions. As an investor, we would want our distributions to be the result of real returns made by the fund and not merely a return of our capital resulting in erosion of NAV.

Valuation and Price to NAV

The current net asset value ("NAV") for HHIC ETF is almost identical to its market price. As it appears in the chart, the relationship between market price and NAV has remained largely aligned since inception. Therefore, at present the fund does not appear to be trading at any meaningful discount to NAV that might otherwise offer an additional opportunity for investors to benefit from discount narrowing. However, the chart also suggests that another comparable Harvest product, Harvest Diversified High Income Shares ETF, has also traded and continues to trade identically to its NAV as well, suggesting that this price-NAV relationship being identical may continue going forward.

In simple terms, investors should expect total return to be largely driven by the distributions received and changes in the fund’s net asset value over time. However, it would still be prudent to allow for the possibility that the fund may trade at some discount to NAV at times in the future.

Noteworthy Factors & Considerations

I recently covered the YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF and commented (TSLY) on why it doesn't make much sense for either income-oriented or capital appreciation-focused investors. If I now compare the Harvest Canadian High Income Shares ETF with the TSLY ETF, many of the deficiencies that existed due to the nature of a single-stock ETF appear to be addressed in this fund.

Firstly, there is a stated 50% cap on the option overlay at the portfolio level. In addition, the fund holds a basket of stocks rather than a single underlying, which largely mitigates the problem of materially reduced upside. Since not all securities in the portfolio would advance above the written strike levels at the same time, even if one or two holdings do so, the 50% overwrite limit still preserves meaningful upside participation. Furthermore, even if some holdings are partially called away, the overall portfolio-level upside would not be significantly affected, unlike the single-stock ETFs similar to TSLY.

The other important factor is that the modest leverage enhances portfolio returns in two ways. A certain portion of the written calls would effectively be attributed to the leveraged exposure, meaning the upside on that portion is not entirely capped but rather enhanced through the additional equity exposure. This incremental return may partly offset the upside that is capped on the unleveraged portion of the portfolio.

Secondly, leverage allows the fund to write a greater number of option contracts relative to its net asset value, since the option-writing limit is determined at the portfolio exposure level rather than strictly on NAV. Thus, the modest leverage essentially enhances the income potential and attractiveness of the option overlay, even though the strategy is not explicitly marketed around the same. Nevertheless, the issue that still persists is that in case of a price decline of the underlying, the leverage would amplify the losses.

Put differently, in HHIC, an investor needs to focus more on managing the downside rather than worrying about the upside. In the end, the objective of the fund remains to generate additional income and returns relative to broader indices such as the Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index, rather than to track a particular security.

We can also check the volatility levels of the underlying holdings as a reference and see what prospects Option Overlay suggests for the time being.

Without going into excessive bifurcations, the above chart broadly suggests that the current volatility environment is supportive for the fund’s option-writing strategy, particularly due to the higher volatility present in commodity-linked holdings. The elevated volatility in stocks such as Agnico Eagle Mines Limited and Cameco Corporation, along with relatively stable volatility in the banking holdings, suggests a favorable deployment environment for the strategy. In other words, high volatility in commodities tends to generate large premiums, while moderate volatility in banks/others helps stabilize the portfolio. Although the outcome would also remain contingent on the portfolio manager’s execution.

Commodity Tailwinds Supporting Canadian Equities

As I mentioned in the beginning, the Canadian economy, and as a result equities, tends to benefit from the uptrend in commodities. This is further supported by the fact that Canadian equities significantly outperformed the S&P 500 and Dow Jones over the last year, when we saw a significant increase in commodity prices. This also has a spillover effect — increased cash flows from these sectors result in an increase in overall economic activity across the region, thus being beneficial not only for companies directly involved but also for other sectors as well indirectly.

It is also worth noting that crude prices are up by almost around 50% so far in FY26. While the exact earnings impact is not yet visible, it would not be surprising to see Canadian energy companies, particularly firms such as Canadian Natural Resources or Suncor Energy, which are also part of HHIC:CA, benefit materially from the same. The recent share price appreciation reflects this assertion.

At the same time, HHIC:CA has seen an increase in market price/NAV of around 10% over the same period. This is merely intended to illustrate that Canadian equities, by nature, tend to benefit from uptrends in commodities and energy, which in turn naturally gets reflected in ETFs focusing on Canadian equities, such as HHIC. This, in no way, suggests any linear correlation between these variables and is only meant to help visualize the broader point. The depiction simply highlights that periods of commodity and energy strength often coincide with relative outperformance of Canadian equities and ETFs focused on them — versus broad-based U.S. indices.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration ("EIA") has also materially revised its Energy price forecast for FY26 and FY27.

Other similar institutions have also been raising their outlook targets. Put simply, it might be reasonable to suggest that energy prices might most probably settle in FY26 on a higher trajectory than where they began. This would place investors in HHIC in a relatively more favorable position compared with broad-based ETFs such as SPY.

It should at the same time be kept in mind that these are contingent factors, and expectations may very well reverse as well. Nonetheless, the current macro trajectory appears more supportive of the investment proposition in the fund than otherwise.

Risks

Foremost, the leverage can amplify drawdowns arising from a decline in the price of the underlying holdings. The ETF prospectus does not indicate the use of protective put options to hedge downside risk, which leaves the portfolio largely exposed to equity market movements. Also, even though the option overlay is generally mandated to be around 50% of the portfolio, it still has the potential to meaningfully reduce upside participation if the underlying securities appreciate significantly beyond the strike prices of the written call options. In addition, the fund has a very limited operating history, and its current performance appears to be supported by a favorable commodity cycle. If the commodity cycle were to reverse, the fund could underperform broader equity indices, with the effect potentially magnified by the use of leverage. The severe reversal in commodities/energies has the potential to lose the gains accumulated so far.

Like many distribution-oriented ETFs, there is also the possibility that the monthly distributions may come from return of capital rather than from portfolio income, leading to periodic erosion of net asset value. Therefore, the strategy may remain suitable primarily for investors seeking higher distribution yields while being willing to tolerate greater volatility and the possibility of sharper drawdowns.

Further, the portfolio managers security selection decisions will play a greater role in determining the fund’s long-term performance trajectory for this ETF.

Takeaway

The Harvest Canadian High-Income Shares ETF provides an interesting vehicle for investors seeking monthly income while maintaining exposure to commodity- and resource-linked equities. Given the current macro backdrop, there remains a reasonable possibility that the commodity cycle might continue further, and periodic spikes in resource prices cannot be entirely ruled out. The fund’s exposure to companies directly benefiting from such cycles, combined with incremental income generated through option premiums, makes the strategy a potentially compelling proposition.

The moderate leverage of roughly 25% does not appear excessive, and the premiums written through covered calls tend to partially offset the effect of leverage. Further, the leveraged nature of the fund allows the fund to write options on a larger number of contracts than would be possible if the portfolio had been unleveraged. Simply put, while the fund maintains a 50% overwrite limit at the portfolio level, the use of leverage increases the effective option-writing capacity relative to the fund’s net asset value.

Therefore, the fund may be viewed favorably for investors with moderate to high-risk appetites seeking higher income and potential capital appreciation. The current positioning of the portfolio, together with the use of leverage and option overlay, provides a reasonable possibility for the distribution yield to remain attractive while also allowing for some degree of price appreciation if the commodity and resource outlook remains good.

data.title: The Market Turned On Arbor Realty, But The Series D Preferreds May Spell Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Arbor Realty Trust, Inc. (ABR) has been suffering some portfolio setbacks, manifested in higher delinquencies, loan extensions, and modifications. The company is still profitable, and given its current valuation, I am bullish on the common shares, conditional on management continuing its progress in cleaning up the portfolio.

For a better safety buffer, ABR's preferreds offer a better alternative to the common shares. Equally important, they trade at a discount to liquidation preference, offering a capital appreciation potential to complement the attractive dividend yield. Series D Preferreds (ABR.PR.D) are cheaper than ABR's Series F Preferreds (ABR.PR.F), giving them a larger capital gain potential if ABR's core business stabilizes.

Series D Preferred Features

Unlike Series F, Series D doesn't carry a rate reset feature. The rate is fixed, which could mean price volatility as market rates change. In terms of yield, Series D now offers 9.13% based on today's price of $17.46/share. The shares are cumulative, meaning that if ABR misses a payment, it must halt all common dividend distributions until the preferreds are current. That's a particularly valuable feature, particularly given the sensitive position ABR is in, where incentives could clash. Common dividends have been cut (and most likely will be cut again this year), as management continues its effort to stabilize the business.

How Bad Is The Core Business

ABR's challenges are serious. Net income stood at $107 million in FY'25, down 52% YoY, as net interest income declined on the back of higher foreclosures, delinquencies, and loan modifications that, in some instances, carried lower pay rates. In FY'25, the company reversed $29.3 million of accrued interest income on modified loans as collection became more uncertain (p.75). By December 2025, ABR held $499 million in foreclosed properties (p.53), up from $177 million in the prior year. Non-performing loans stood at $545 million, a significant amount when juxtaposed against a 12 billion portfolio.

Still, one needs to put all these challenges into perspective. I believe that ABR will cycle through its troubled portfolio. Origination remains strong. Troubled assets are mainly older vintages. Then, one needs to take into consideration that a chunk of the earnings decline is because of the capital tied to the foreclosed properties and delinquent loans. The situation is not ideal, but a foreclosed property is typically liquidated, and the capital is recycled. Maybe this won't happen next quarter, and the pressure on earnings will continue longer. However, eventually, I believe ABR will emerge, possibly stronger, from its current situation.

Ample Preferred Coverage

There are three classes of preferreds. Series D preferreds rank in pari passu with the others, and the entire class has priority claim on distribution over common shares but is subordinate to the lenders. For this reason, in this section alone, I'll refer to the preferreds as a collective class when discussing the preferred dividend safety. Otherwise, this article is primarily focused on Series D preferreds.

ABR's profitability has been impacted by the factors discussed above. However, common equity is the first to take the hit, and it seems there is a comfortable buffer, both in terms of profitability/dividend and liquidation, if push comes to shove.

2025 2024 2023
Distributable earnings ($000s) $223,570 $358,020 $452,479
Diluted weighted average shares outstanding 209,733,331 205,526,610 201,549,221
Diluted distributable earnings per share $1.07 $1.74 $2.25

Distributable earnings, management's preferred performance metric to assess payout, stood at 223.6 million in FY'25. That's below the $358 million reported in the prior year. However, there are two points that are important to put the figure into perspective in the context of our discussion on Series D preferreds.

First, the distributable earnings figure is net of interest expense ($702 million) and preferred distributions ($41.4 million). The earnings available to common stockholders are the earnings available for common shareholders after the obligations on more senior securities are paid. In my view, $223.5 million is a comfortable buffer for the preferred equity holders, given that the entire class carries a preferred distribution obligation of just $41.4 million. In other words, the $223.6 million distributable earnings translate to a pre-preferred distribution coverage of 6.4x.

Why Series D?

In my view, the best two options for a capital appreciation plus fixed income trade are Series D and Series E (ABR.PR.E), but not necessarily Series F, the latter being more aligned with an interest-rate hedging strategy. Series D and Series E have almost the same price, trading at $17.46 and $17.36, respectively. That's a 30.2% and 30.6% discount on the preferred liquidation preference for both securities, which stands at $25/share. Series F trades close to $22.2/share, a lower discount compared to the other two series.

The liquidation preference is the price the investors receive when these preferreds are called, at the option of ABR. Preferreds D preferreds are perpetual, with no maturity date, meaning that there are only two routes to exit this trade: 1) sell the preferreds in the open market, or 2) in the event that ABR calls the preferreds at $25/share. Series D is callable starting June 2026, but I doubt that ABR will call its preferreds given how much risk the market is assigning to its securities. Still, if Series D preferreds were to revert back to the 25/share market, the capital appreciation opportunity is 43.2%, complementing a 9.1% dividend yield at the time of this writing.

Final Thoughts And Risks

The main risk to this thesis is that ABR's credit troubles prove deeper than implied here. I believe that the earnings buffer of 6.4x distributable earnings on preferred distributions is pretty good. The profitability trend has been downward, so this argument weakens a bit.

Still, overall, I believe that management's efforts to stabilize the business are working, as evident in the lower non-performing loans compared to the prior year (although this perhaps is, at least partly, tied to the larger foreclosures). Loan modifications are lower as well compared to FY'24. The biggest red flag is the recently priced senior notes. The notes carry a yield comparable to the preferreds. For example, in December 2025, ABR priced $400 million in senior notes due 2028 at 8.5%. That's only 0.63% lower than the Series D yield at today's price.

It's hard to assign a relative valuation, given the competing dynamics attributed to the different features of the two fixed-income securities. The notes are senior and have a defined maturity date, unlike Series D preferreds; thus, a lower yield is to be expected. But, at the same time, the preferreds enjoy a liquidity premium, given that, unlike the senior notes, they are traded on a public market. I would have been more confident in this Buy rating on Series D preferreds had the notes been priced at a lower yield.

data.title: Oil Is The New Silver; Time To Short?
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Oil’s (USO) current run is reminiscent of what we saw take place in the silver market only a few weeks ago.

Back then, I highlighted the dangers of shorting silver while also warning that a potential top was likely in place.

Today, oil offers us a similar set-up, with an added benefit.

While there was definitely an element of structural supply-demand imbalance in silver, oil prices have been structurally depressed over the last year.

This rally has been caused by an exogenous factor, and if/once this clears, oil will go back to much lower prices.

With silver, we were trying to time a market that was inherently strong but had gotten ahead of itself.

But with oil, we’re looking at shorting a market that wants to head lower.

How We ‘Called’ The Silver Top

Just a few weeks ago, it seemed everyone was looking at silver, which had run up over 300% in a year.

There were plenty of reasons for this, including supply-demand imbalance, price inelasticity, and also a degree of speculation.

Back on January 27, I told investors silver could indeed reach $200, but first I expected a large pull-back.

This call was made due to a variety of different factors, but mainly sentiment, which had become overly bullish, and technicals.

As we can see, silver had built a very large bearish divergence with the RSI, which was still overbought on the daily time frame.

At the same time, we saw a bearish shooting star candle.

Zooming into the 4h chart, there was also a very clear double top with yet another bearish divergence in the RSI.

That's not to say this works every time, but the indications were there.

Is Oil Giving Us A Similar Set-Up?

Oil is now getting interesting and potentially giving us a similar technical setup.

First off, if we look at the weekly, we are already in very overbought territory.

On top of that, we already saw a pretty bearish shooting star candle take place back on Monday, as oil prices began to strongly reverse.

Since then, it looks like things have heated up a bit, and this war may not actually end as soon as some want it to.

Now, zooming into the 4h chart, we can see that after a sharp move lower, we’re attempting to break to new highs.

If we did get a higher high, this could potentially give us a similar set-up to silver with a potential double top and a bearish RSI divergence at overbought levels.

Geopolitics Is Pushing Us Higher, For Now

The ongoing war in Iran has severely disrupted flows through the Strait of Hormuz, which accounts for 20% of the world’s oil supply.

While there was speculation earlier in the week about the situation stabilizing and boats getting escorted through the Strait, this was later walked back by the US Navy.

If anything, things have been getting worse, with boats now being directly targeted. And as this situation continues, it also hits production, which has to be stopped due to a lack of storage space.

Production across the Gulf has been cut by millions of barrels per day, pushing oil back toward $100 per barrel.

President Trump has claimed the war will end soon, but the current evidence on the ground seems to go against this.

Fundamentals Will Take Us Back Down, Eventually

But ultimately, things will normalize, and when that happens, oil will quickly head back down. Remember that before this, oil had been trading at historically low prices for quite some time.

Lower oil prices are a priority for the U.S. administration and something that is supported by current demand dynamics.

The IEA itself said oil would be oversupplied in 2026, and this makes sense given the current move away from oil, the increase in renewables, and also Electric Vehicles.

Plus, oil drilling has become much more efficient in recent years.

The long-term story for oil is a bearish one.

Final Thoughts

Although risky, I think a new high in oil could potentially be a great shorting opportunity. Unlike with silver, the fundamentals are pushing us down, and they will eventually win.

In the short term, this will depend on the fundamentals of the war, which is why picking an exact top might be hard.

However, if we see a technical setup like a highlight sometime next week, then I might consider entering a short trade, and I’d definitely cut down on oil exposure.

data.title: Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings: A Great Business At A Lofty Price
data.description:
March 12th was a pretty good day for shareholders of Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings, Inc. (OLLI), a niche player in the retail space that has, operationally speaking, done exceptionally well over the last few years. Its emphasis on closeout merchandise and excess inventory in the U.S. has cemented it as an attractive investment opportunity in the eyes of many investors. So you can imagine that many of them were likely happy to see the stock trade up about 3.5% in the afternoon hours after management announced financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. In addition to revenue and profits coming in significantly above what analysts anticipated, management projected out financial results for 2026 that point toward even more growth. This is fantastic to see, but the downside is that shares of the business remain horribly expensive.

For context, back in early December of last year, I reaffirmed Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings as a Sell candidate. Even though I acknowledged the quality of the company, I was turned off by how expensive the stock had been. I know that investors were pricing future growth. That that can only take you so far in terms of valuation.

Since that time, my call has proven to be successful. Shares are down 11.7%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is down only 1.7%. And since I originally downgraded it from a Hold to a Sell back in May of last year, the stock is down 3.1% compared to the 17.8% increase that the market saw. To be clear, from a valuation standpoint, the stock is more attractive than it was back then. But it's still not cheap enough to justify an upgrade right now. For now, I think that maintaining it as a soft ‘sell’ is the appropriate choice.

A Great Quarter

By all accounts, the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was a great time for shareholders of Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings. Or at least that's the case when it comes to the fundamentals of the business. Revenue for the company came in strong at $779.3 million. That's an increase of 16.8% over the $667.1 million in revenue generated a year earlier. Some of this increase came from a rise in comparable store sales of 3.6%. Management chalked this improvement up to an increase in how much customers were spending per transaction and to a rise in the number of transactions.

There were multiple categories that did exceptionally well. Examples would include seasonal products, consumables, hardware, stationery, and sporting goods. In their final quarter earnings call, the management team at the business provided significant additional detail. When it comes to seasonal offerings, for instance, management made significant changes, specifically centered around offering more seasonal decor. The toy market continues to change as well as consumers move away from traditional products and toward interactive ones. This clearly is an opportunity for the company if played right. But in addition to this, the company continues to benefit from its Ollie’s Army loyalty program.

That saw membership growth of 23% year over year while its total customer file jumped in excess of 12%. Loyalty programs are incredibly valuable because customers that sign up for them ultimately shop more frequently and spend more than customers that aren't included in them. And when you add on top of this certain initiatives centered around it, such as its Ollie’s Army night that it had in June of last year and the company's decision to make its Ollie’s Days event exclusive to members only, you have a lot of catalysts for growth here. The company has even gone so far as to roll out its own credit card, which offers an opportunity for additional branding and promotional activities.

This is fantastic, but even these changes were not the primary driver of growth. The biggest involved a significant increase in the number of stores that the company has in operation. The firm went from having 559 stores in the final quarter of 2024 to having 645 at the end of last year. That's an increase of 86 stores, or 15.4%, in the course of a single year. Of course, this doesn't come cheap. In the final quarter of the year, for instance, the company also added $18 million toward capital expenditures. The year as a whole saw capital expenditures amount to $101.9 million.

This doesn't mean that the company hasn't been capable of returning cash to shareholders as well. Throughout 2025, the company bought back about $73.8 million worth of stock. $33.6 million of that occurred in the final quarter of the year. And even after all of this, the firm has cash in excess of debt totaling $294.8 million.

The revenue reported by management ended up being $112.2 million above what analysts were hoping to see. But that's not all. Earnings per share jumped from $1.11 to $1.39 in the course of a year. The earnings ultimately exceeded analysts’ estimates to the tune of $0.51. That also meant a rise in net profits from $68.6 million to $85.6 million. On an adjusted basis, the company did incredibly well also. Adjusted earnings per share went from $1.19 to $1.39. This exceeded expectations that analysts had by $0.51 and translated to a rise in adjusted net income from $73.4 million to $85.4 million.

Cash flows also increased. Operating cash flow, as an example, jumped from $147.8 million to $182.4 million. Normally I would like to look at adjusted operating cash flow as well, which strips out changes in working capital. That management has not provided enough detail to make this calculation just yet. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company expanded from $109.4 million to $127.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2025 in their entirety compared to 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows all grew rapidly during this time. The aforementioned increase in the number of locations in operation played a big role here. But on top of this, the company enjoyed a 3.7% increase in comparable store sales. What's really exciting here is that we should expect even more growth in 2026. Management intends to allocate between $103 million and $113 million toward capital initiatives. This will allow the company to open around 75 new stores, which, when combined with a comparable store sales growth of 2%, should push revenue up to between $2.985 billion and $3.015 billion. At the midpoint, that would be 13.2% above the $2.65 billion the company reported for 2025.

When it comes to store counts, one thing that is worth noting is that the company is pushing into new geographic regions. In fact, on March 5th, the firm announced that it had entered into its 35th state, which is Minnesota, with a store opening there.

From this sales increase, we should get adjusted net profits of between $270 million and $277 million. Hitting the midpoint there would mean a year-over-year jump in adjusted net income of 14.7%. If we assume that other profitability metrics will rise similarly, we would get operating cash flow of $340 million and EBITDA of $419.7 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart below how shares of the company are priced right now. On a forward basis, the stock actually doesn't look that bad. I would put it at the higher end of the fair value range. But when it comes to the 2025 multiples, which would be most appropriate to look at considering where we are in the calendar and fiscal year, shares are still very pricey.

Takeaway

At this point in time, I would be crazy to say that Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings is anything other than a fantastic business that is doing a remarkable job. I suspect that, for years to come, growth like this will continue. Having said that, there are limitations to how much investors should pay for growth. And right now, I think that shares are still overpriced.

If we see the stock remain more or less around the level that it's at today, and if management ultimately achieves their targets for 2026, I think upgrading it to a Hold will be appropriate. But for now, a bearish assessment is justified.

data.title: Medical Properties Trust: High Safety Margin
data.description:
Medical Properties (MPT) engineered a reversal in its net profits in 2025 amid a broad-based portfolio restructuring, and the hospital REIT fully supported its dividend with normalized funds from operations in the fourth quarter. The hospital investor has also made considerable progress resolving its exposure to the Prospect Medical Holdings bankruptcy, which is limiting risks for investors and could potentially set the REIT up for normalized FFO growth in 2026.

With more positive news coming from Medical Properties, including a 12.5% dividend increase, I believe Medical Properties could be a promising turnaround investment in 2026. Most importantly, shares are really cheap, based off normalized FFO and book value, which translates to a risk profile that is skewed to the upside.

Previous rating

I rated Medical Properties a "Strong (speculative) Buy" last year, mainly because I felt that the REIT was overly punished by investors after it lost its anchor tenant, Steward Health Care, to bankruptcy in 2024 and sold off assets to pay down its considerable debt: 1.75X Coverage, Stabilizing FFO, Re-Pricing Catalyst.

Medical Properties made progress in restructuring its portfolio, and the hospital REIT fully earned its dividend payout (which was also raised) in the last quarter. I like that MPT is moving forward with asset sales and debt repayments, and 2026 could be a major transition year for the company.

Stable debt situation, stabilizing portfolio

The REIT's asset divestment strategy in 2024 and 2025 was driven by a loss of key tenants—such as Steward Health Care and Prospect Medical, previously important tenants—and a desire to refocus on a stabilization of Medical Properties' cash flows. While the company sold more than $3.0 billion in assets in 2024, Medical Properties most recently returned to select portfolio acquisitions. As of the end of the most recent quarter, Medical Properties carried $9.7 billion in long-term debt (including drawn-down credit facilities), highlighting 1% quarter-over-quarter growth.

In terms of portfolio, Medical Properties owned 384 properties as of the end of the December quarter, showing a year-over-year decline of 12 units. The decline in the asset base is part of the REIT's asset monetization strategy and resulted in the shedding of non-core or distressed assets. In the fourth quarter, Medical Properties sold a total of 6 properties as it is transitioning medical facilities involved in the Prospect Medical restructuring. Prospect Medical filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in January 2025, but reached an out-of-court restructuring agreement with MPT last year amid persistent challenges in its core healthcare market, resulting in Medical Properties transitioning its properties to other operators.

Medical Properties has been busy exiting several Prospect Medical-related properties in the fourth quarter, including two facilities in Phoenix, Arizona, for $50 million at the end of 2025, pursuant to a tenant purchase option. In addition, two hospitals in Connecticut were sold to Hartford HealthCare for $86.1 million, also at the end of 2025. Further, Medical Properties re-tenanted six hospitals in California to NOR Healthcare. The new 15-year lease term for the six hospitals in California is expected to yield ~$45 million in annual rent by the end of the current year, stabilize the portfolio, and lead to NFFO visibility.

Fully covered dividend payout, raised dividend

Medical Properties generated $106.6 million in normalized funds from operations in the fourth quarter, showing a mild 1% decline year-over-year. The stabilization of the REIT's NFFO, which dropped off especially in 2024 amid an accelerating pace of asset sales, is the main reason why I still like the REIT.

On a per-share basis, Medical Properties generated $0.18 in normalized funds from operations in Q4'25, which calculates, given a $0.09 per-share dividend payout, to a dividend coverage ratio of 200%. For the full year, the REIT reported $0.58 in normalized FFO, which implies a dividend coverage ratio of 176%. Both dividend coverage ratios, for Q4 and FY 2025, imply very low dividend risks for investors, in my opinion. The fact that the REIT raised its Q4'25 dividend by 12.5% to $0.09 per share, up from $0.08 per share quarterly previously, is a vote of confidence in the REIT's cash flow trajectory.

High margin of safety

Even under the very conservative assumption that the hospital REIT sees no real NFFO growth in 2026, Medical Properties is still making a strong value proposition for investors. With the REIT saying that it stabilized its real estate assets, I believe investors are too negative about Medical Properties. Most importantly, the REIT just raised its dividend, and there is no better sign to signal growing confidence in a company's growth prospects than by raising the payout, which is seen as a permanent commitment to shareholders.

Currently, shares of Medical Properties, assuming stable normalized funds from operations, are trading at a forward price-to-NFFO ratio of 8.4X, which implies a high margin of safety for investors. From a book value perspective, MPT is a value deal as well, trading at just about 0.63X of its book value.

While shares are slightly more expensive than they were on average in the last 3 years, they are still trading at a significant markdown from book value... which is a conservative estimate of fair value for large REITs. A revaluation to ~0.7-0.8X book value is possible in 2026, in my opinion, assuming that MPT continues to stabilize its investment portfolio and pays back more debt. This valuation range implies a fair value for shares of Medical Properties of $5.40-$6.18.

Risks with MPT

The risks with regard to Medical Properties are two-fold: 1) The REIT still carries a lot of debt on its balance sheet, which management must address to ensure a revaluation to the upside, and 2) MPT still needs to return to more formidable NFFO growth. MPT could potentially achieve this by selectively growing its core real estate portfolio, although the REIT would likely see an increase in its debt again. What would change my mind about MPT is if the REIT were to see a contraction in its dividend coverage ratio to less than 150%.

Final thoughts

Many long-term investors have been burned with Medical Properties as the REIT embarked on a major restructuring plan in 2024, following a debt-fueled acquisition spree that ultimately forced the REIT to lower its dividend and downsize its real estate portfolio. Medical Properties, however, has made considerable progress in recent years, especially in 2025, which is when it stabilized its normalized funds from operations. Recent asset transitions—especially the six properties referenced above in California—highlight restructuring progress and ultimately lower risks for investors. Additionally, the 12.5% increase in the dividend as well as the strong dividend coverage ratio, both for Q4 and FY 2025, highlight a well-supported payout that is not at risk of a near-term adjustment, in my opinion.

data.title: QYLD: A 12% Yield With Structural Flaws
data.description:
The Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) boasts an almost 12% yield. But does this make it a good income-generating investment long-term? In this analysis, I want to explain why QYLD’s distributions and total returns behaved a certain way. This ETF's portfolio construction is transparent. But it does not mean that it works on the Nasdaq-100. Historically, QYLD has not produced a favorable result for income-seeking investors. Other similar covered call ETFs do a better job by mitigating QYLD’s structural issues.

QYLD Portfolio Construction in a Nutshell

QYLD has been around since 2013. It is one of the oldest covered call ETFs that is appealing to income investors. The concept behind it is relatively simple to understand. The fund buys the Nasdaq-100 and then sells at-the-money call options on it on a monthly basis. At-the-money options generate income that QYLD distributes to investors on a monthly basis.

The fund has a rule that it does not distribute all of the premium income received. Instead, QYLD distributes 50% of the premiums received or 1% of the fund’s net asset value, whichever is lower. The remaining balance gets reinvested back into the fund.

Why QYLD’s Covered Call Strategy Does Not Work on Nasdaq-100

Here is why QYLD’s investment process does not work on the Nasdaq-100, in my opinion. The Nasdaq-100 is very volatile and growth-oriented. It can make strong moves to both the upside and the downside. The most important job of a covered call ETF is to capture as much of that upside while keeping its downside low.

To capture this upside, covered call funds have two main levers to pull. First is portfolio coverage by sold call options. If a fund sells call options on, say, 50% of its stock portfolio, it can capture future upside on the remaining 50%. Many covered call ETFs like QQQI, JEPQ, and GPIQ do not sell call options on their entire portfolios.

Conversely, QYLD writes call options on 100% of its portfolio. The upshot is that it captures a lot of premium income in return. But the downside is big. 100% portfolio coverage hamstrings a lot of future upside of the Nasdaq-100.

It may seem okay for an investor looking for steady income. But this approach creates a big problem for QYLD long-term, as we’ll see shortly. QYLD’s income is far from steady, partly due to this 100% portfolio coverage.

The second lever is about choosing the strike price for sold call options. Strike prices above the current Nasdaq-100 price give more room for upside capture. But the farther out the strike price, the smaller the premium is. The converse is true. It is a balance that many covered call funds strive to strike. Many Nasdaq-100 covered call funds chose to sell out-of-the-money call options instead.

What about QYLD? It sells at-the-money call options. This generates plenty of income. But the upside is capped at the option premium earned. This is what the chart below shows.

The above picture shows a 4% monthly premium on the Nasdaq-100. Historically, it has been lower, probably somewhere close to 3%. What this means is that if the Nasdaq-100 deviates by this much on up- or downswings in a given month, QYLD suffers. If the Nasdaq-100 jumps over 3%, all future upside is capped. Investors won’t see a dime of that. This creates an opportunity cost of foregone upside.

That could be fine with investors who don’t care about growth but only income. And this is where the problem arises. When the Nasdaq-100 goes down by more than 3% in a month, there will be no premium income to shield QYLD from it.

Here is a histogram that shows the Nasdaq-100’s monthly price returns since December 2013, when QYLD began trading.

We see that the Nasdaq-100 made plenty of monthly upside moves above 3%. They account for 38% of all months since December 2013. The foregone upside is huge. But look at the left tail of this distribution where the downside sits. That tail is pretty fat, so to speak. Monthly price moves below 3% accounted for 15% of all months.

While the frequency of bad months matters, their size is even more important. 2022 was terrible for the Nasdaq-100. The monthly declines were 13% and 10% in April and September alone.

When these bad months happen, QYLD captures plenty of downside beyond its 3% or so premium income. But, when recovery ensues, QYLD gets stuck. As it repeats its monthly call option selling, it resets its upside cap at a lower strike price on the Nasdaq-100. So, when the Nasdaq-100 recovers, QYLD continues staying down for long.

QYLD Structural Issues = Poor Capture Ratios

And this is where capture ratios come in handy. They help me to sift through the marketing hype that covered call ETFs put out in front of investors. All they mean is to what extent a given fund captures the downside or upside of its benchmark in a given period. For instance, if the Nasdaq-100 goes up by 10% and QYLD captures only half of that, the upside capture ratio is 50%. The same goes for the downside capture ratio.

The best outcome for a fund is to have upside capture as high as possible and downside capture as low as possible. The worst outcome is when upside capture is below the downside capture ratio. In other words, an ETF takes a lot of beating in bear markets but is very slow to recover afterward. At the same time, you don’t want your upside capture to be too low. If it is too low, the fund’s income may stagnate.

Here is a table showing capture ratios for 4 popular covered call ETFs focusing on the Nasdaq-100.

JEPQ GPIQ QQQI QYLD
Upside Capture 77.64% 87.49% 82.48% 48.22%
Downside Capture 67.35% 78.19% 69.55% 28.64%

Source: Portfolio Visualizer (Feb 2024 - Feb 2026)

We see that QYLD has the lowest upside capture of the four funds. But its downside capture is very low too. So, while it did not go up by as much, when everything fell in 2024-2026, QYLD fell less. But, even so, QYLD managed to lag its peers on a total return and volatility-adjusted basis. Its low upside capture ratio was detrimental for its performance.

Metric JEPQ GPIQ QQQI QYLD Invesco QQQ Trust
Start Balance $10,000.00 $10,000.00 $10,000.00 $10,000.00 $10,000.00
End Balance $14,058.00 $14,483.00 $14,370.00 $12,903.00 $14,724.00
Annualized Return (CAGR) 17.76% 19.46% 19.01% 13.01% 20.41%
Standard Deviation 10.03% 11.25% 10.31% 7.32% 13.55%
Best Year 21.24% 20.86% 21.56% 16.26% 23.33%
Worst Year 0.65% 0.04% -0.34% 1.59% -1.14%
Maximum Drawdown -8.38% -8.77% -8.23% -9.14% -10.09%
Sharpe Ratio 1.24 1.25 1.31 1.1 1.11
Sortino Ratio 2 2.2 2.28 1.53 2.05
Benchmark Correlation 0.93 0.99 0.99 0.75 1

Source: Portfolio Visualizer (Feb 2024 - Feb 2026)

Let’s expand this time window. After all, there were no protracted bear episodes like in 2022. For the last 10 years, QYLD’s downside capture ratio was 62%. But the upside capture was lower at only ~50%. This is not a good outcome for a covered call fund.

QYLD’s Poor Capture Ratios = Poor Distributions

Poor capture ratios show up in QYLD's NAV erosion as approximated by QYLD’s stock price. 2022 was especially a bad year. That episode led to severe NAV erosion, as can be seen from the QYLD stock price.

In fact, without its policy of distributing only half of its premium income, QYLD’s NAV erosion would have been much worse.

Why is stable and growing NAV per share so important for QYLD or any other covered call fund? Because without it, the fund won’t have a capital base to sell options and sustain its distributions.

As NAV erosion took hold of QYLD, its distributions got nowhere in the last 10 years (with a few exceptions). If we adjust these distributions for inflation, QYLD's income has stagnated.

QYLD: Not a Good Choice in the 2026 Environment

The most favorable scenario for QYLD is when the Nasdaq-100 stays strictly within its monthly premium income cushion of about 3%. So far, 2026 has proven to be tame.

The Nasdaq-100 monthly fluctuations were within an acceptable range of -2.6% to 3.2%. However, the Nasdaq-100 has to stay that way until the third Friday of each month, when QYLD's written option on the Nasdaq-100 expires.

For instance, here is the most recent QYLD's disclosure on its call option:

QYLD uses European-style options that cannot be executed by its holder until the contract expires. These contracts are cash settled instead at the end. The last written option on February 20 with a strike price of 25,075 expires on March 20. Any deviations of ~3% on March 20 from this price would produce either losses or upside caps.

Unlike other funds, QYLD does not stagger its written options. It is a monthly one-shot type of arrangement with one written option on 100% of its portfolio. That's a big drawback in the 2026 environment. The armed conflict with Iran, oil price spikes, and fears around AI-focused stocks are producing a lot of volatility that may be here to stay for the rest of the year. QYLD's process for writing call options is too rigid in my opinion.

Other funds like JEPQ and GPIQ ladder their covered call positions on a weekly basis. This gives them more flexibility in a fast-changing environment not to get caught in the crossfires of news flow. Even though the Nasdaq-100 remains relatively flat, it still fluctuates. If it happens to make a quick downward move on the third Friday of March only to recover later, QYLD may get locked into a loss with a subsequent upside cap.

Even though I am bullish on the Nasdaq-100 long-term, 2026 may be a year of continued volatility and consolidation. While QYLD's bigger option premium is good in these markets, its 100% portfolio coverage coupled with once-a-month option writing does not give enough flexibility to effectively monetize Nasdaq-100 fluctuations.

Investor Takeaways

I rarely rate a fund a SELL. Most ETFs have their place and time. But QYLD’s structural flaws make me question it as a sustainable long-term holding for income investors. Arguably, better options exist on the market with covered call overlay on the Nasdaq-100.

QYLD’s covered call overlay would probably work best on some other tame index. If this index stays flat with no fluctuations outside its safe premium income zone, no problem. This is hardly the case for the Nasdaq-100.

QYLD is not a fixed-income fund. Its returns and distributions come with an equity-style risk. But, if you layer a wrong covered call approach on top of that equity risk, its investment risk goes up by even more.

The most favorable scenario for QYLD is when the Nasdaq-100 stays strictly within its monthly premium income cushion of about 3%. Since its inception, this has happened roughly 47% of the time.

On top of it, strong downward moves present the biggest problem for QYLD. Once they happen, the fund will struggle to repair the damage. If the Nasdaq-100 swings down by 3% or more, QYLD’s NAV erosion will lead to income cuts. It does not take a very big bear episode. As long as this episode shows monthly losses above 3-4% for 6 months or longer, QYLD will struggle.

With the Nasdaq-100, you either have to vary portfolio coverage like what GPIQ does or give it some breathing room by selling out-of-the-money call options like what JEPQ and QQQI do. These funds tend to do a better job of protecting themselves against NAV erosion and declining income.

data.title: GLTR: Four Precious Metals In One ETF
data.description:
Introduction to the abrdn Physical Precious Metals Basket Shares ETF

The abrdn Physical Precious Metals Basket Shares ETF (GLTR), which began trading on October 21, 2010, is backed by the investment management group Aberdeen Group PLC. GLTR, which has amassed total assets under management of $3.35B, is priced at $245 and can be accessed at an expense ratio of 0.6%. GLTR does not pay any dividends to its holders.

What Does GLTR Do?

GLTR’s remit is to serve as a convenient one-stop shop for those who want to bet on the price movements of four precious metals in physical form- gold, silver, platinum, and palladium—without worrying about the hassles of owning those metals. GLTR, which is set up as a grantor trust, isn’t an equally weighted portfolio but rather holds different proportions of these physical metals (it is over-indexed to physical gold, which alone accounts for 57% of the portfolio) that are parked in vaults based in London.

Unlike most ETFs that track a specific index or resort to active management strategies, GLTR’s purpose is just to own these physical metals and issue shares against its holdings.

Who Are the Main Intermediaries Within GLTR’s Setup?

In order to enable GLTR’s smooth functioning, we have quite a few different players that carry out important roles:

  • Sponsor- The most important role in this setup is carried out by abrdn ETFs Sponsor LLC [AESL], who registers, creates, and issues shares of the product while also selecting the custodian, trustee, inspector, etc.
  • Trustee- The role of the trustee is to carry out and manage the standard administrative work, such as accounting, tax, NAV calculation, etc. This is carried out by Bank of New York Mellon.
  • Custodian- The physical metals that come under GLTR aren’t held by AESL but by a London-based entity called ICBC Standard Bank PLC, which holds these metals in their vaults.
  • Inspector- To verify if the metal held in these vaults is still of the same quality, AESL also relies on Bureau Veritas, which carries out inspections twice a year, with one of the semi-annual inspections taking place any time during a calendar year and the second during the end of the year.

What Are the Risks Associated With GLTR?

As noted earlier, even though GLTR covers four different precious metals, there’s no doubt that it tilts very heavily towards gold (57%), and this may not always be favorable. Unlike Silver and the other industrial precious metals that are also extensively used in industrial applications and in a pro-growth environment, gold is seen as an unproductive asset that also fails to attract meaningful positive interest when the economic cycle turns for the good.

Prima facie, investors may be tempted to believe that since GLTR isn’t a single-commodity ETF and rather focuses on four commodities, the added diversification could bring down the risk profile of this product. The reality, though, is quite different, as GLTR’s annualized volatility profile of 35.24%, which is more than double that of your standard ETF, suggests that this is a product that is prone to seeing wild swings over an annual time frame. This facet makes it a risky proposition for investors with conservative risk appetites.

Given the mammoth quantities of physical metal that GLTR owns, one would think that steps would be taken to insure some part of the portfolio against potential theft, loss, or damages, but that isn’t the case here.

The difference between GLTR’s bid and ask (in percentage terms it works out to 0.22%) suggests that investors run the risk of facing slippages (getting in and out of a position at prices that are different from budgeted prices).

Since GLTR’s structure is that of a grantor trust, it is seen as a pass-through entity, by which all tax consequences are passed on to the holders of GLTR shares. Precious metals held by grantor trusts are also classified as collectibles, making a holding period beyond one year come under a superior collectibles tax rate of 28% if you hold this in a taxable brokerage account. This is a lot more than the standard 15-20% LTCG (long-term capital gains) taxes that standard ETF structures face.

Investors should also consider that since GLTR’s metal holdings don’t produce any cash flow (unlike stocks, which typically generate dividends, or bonds, which generate income), it can only meet its management fees and expense ratio by selling a portion of its holdings every month. Thus, over time, investors of GLTR will see their share of holdings in these metals dip. Also, this monthly sale of metals results in capital gains/losses (which investors don’t get the benefit of) for which investors receive no associated tax breaks.

Who is GLTR For?

A typical proponent of GLTR is a precious metal investor who appreciates the strong safe haven qualities that gold brings, particularly during periods of strife and high inflation (and thus wants to be over indexed to this metal), but also recognizes that when the economic cycle turns, and when global industrial conditions pick up, the other components of this portfolio could pick up the baton (silver is pursued by a range of industries for its high conductive quotient, while palladium and platinum are primarily pursued by the auto industry for their role in emission control).

GLTR would also serve as a useful diversifying tool for those who own equity-heavy portfolios, as it shares a limited correlation with the latter; for instance, over the last decade, GLTR’s correlation with the US equity bellwether has only been around 0.16x, suggesting a very weak linear relationship between these two pockets.

Investors who pursue GLTR are also those who typically aren’t bothered by the income facet of an ETF (something which is very common with ETFs), as its returns are just a function of the price appreciation of the physical holdings, rather than any distributions made.

Given the tax inefficiencies of the structure of this product (discussed in the risk section), we also feel that GLTR would be best suited for those with Roth IRAs [Individual Retirement Accounts].

Those who dislike opaque structures, and rather those who prefer transparency of their holdings on a daily basis, will also appreciate GLTR, as the bar list of this portfolio is updated and made available every day. Also consider that these holdings don’t just lie in the dark forever, with inspections conducted twice a year to verify their quality.

Is There an ETF Alternative to GLTR?

Investors who want an ETF that covers the same precious metals (gold, silver, platinum, and palladium) as GLTR, in physical form, will struggle to find a US-listed alternative. Rather, we feel the closest alternative one could find is the Invesco DB Precious Metals ETF (DBP), although it is not as popular as our product in focus (GLTR’s AUM and daily dollar volumes are 10 and 17x more than DBP) and also has a pricier expense ratio (a 15 bps differential). Both these products typically encounter wide spreads of over 0.2%, although DBP’s is worse by 7 bps.

Now, while DBP’s nomenclature suggests that this product also offers coverage to precious metals, there are quite a few differences to note.

For starters, unlike GLTR, which actually owns and holds quantities of the various precious metals in physical form, DBP does not and is rather a proxy on the futures contracts of certain precious metals

Secondly, while GLTR covers not just the mainstream precious metals of gold and silver but also the industrial precious metal cohort of platinum and palladium, DBP only dabbles with futures contracts of gold and silver and shuns the latter two entirely.

Then we also noted how GLTR does not make any distributions to its holders; DBP differs and makes annual distributions that amount to over 2%, as, besides the futures contracts, it also garners some interest income by holding certain money market instruments and treasury bills.

Summary

GLTR offers convenient and transparent access to those who want to own four precious metals without having to worry about the real-world hassles of owning and maintaining those metals. This gold-heavy portfolio, which is structured as a grantor trust, is best suited for those who have Roth IRAs and don’t have any desire to garner regular income from their stake in GLTR.

This article answers these three main questions about GLTR:

  • Who are the main players behind GLTR?
  • What type of investor is GLTR suitable for, and what are the risks associated with it?
  • How does GLTR differ from a futures-oriented precious metal alternative?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: SILJ: Silver Supercycle Meets War Premium
data.description:
Few funds have ever managed to capture the interest of investors as the Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF (SILJ). After years of volatility, the fund saw an astonishing return of 184% in 2025. The reason for this is the structural shift that has taken place, with silver now becoming an important part of the new AI revolution. Data centers that run AI models require silver for high-performance chips, silver-plated connectors, and switchgear that can distribute power at high voltage. Furthermore, the silver market is now finishing its fifth consecutive year of structural deficit, with the safe-haven status of silver now being compounded by the geopolitical boiling pot.

We will give our overview of the fund, compare it to peer funds, and discuss the ongoing short- and long-term catalysts for the fund and the metal, as well as valuation and potential risks. Let’s go.

The Fund

Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF's main goal is to provide investors exposure to the silver mining industry and companies that extract their revenues from silver mining, silver production, or exploration and development of silver mining sites. The ETF seeks to replicate the performance, before fees and expenses, of the Nasdaq Junior Silver Miners™ Index.

The weighting is based on the market cap of the constituents, with minimum criteria being $20 million and daily turnover criteria of at least $10 thousand over the last three months. The liquidity requirements are fairly loose but expected for the kind of companies and investment vehicles this presents. You can read up more about the index methodology here. These are just some of the more important characteristics. There are 62 holdings in the fund, and the top 10 companies represent around 60% of the fund's assets.

We’ll focus on some key fund characteristics now, using the Seeking Alpha grading system. The fund has a Seeking Alpha C- Expense Grade, weighted down by the higher expense ratio of 69 basis points, higher than the 50 basis points of the median ETF. Regarding liquidity, the fund has a high Seeking Alpha A+ Grade, with above-average daily share and dollar volume, and ample $6.12 billion in assets under management. The fund also has a perfect A+ Seeking Alpha Dividend Grade, driven upwards by the remarkable 3-year annualized growth of 359% and the 10-year annualized growth rate of 50%. The dividend yield, however, is not as impressive, being 1.37% and below the median yield of 2.61%.

The fund is fully equity invested and is naturally concentrated in the Basic Material sector.

The fund offers diversified international exposure while having a significant US portion, with Canada being the most represented country, with around 56% of the fund’s assets, followed by the US with around 30%. Peru and Poland represent the 4th and 5th largest geographical holdings, with 3.94% and 3.7%, respectively.

Compared to peers in the industry, SILJ is lagging only behind iShares MSCI Global Silver and Metals Miners ETF (SLVP) in one category- Expenses, with SLVP being graded with a B-. In terms of the other categories, SILJ is generally best positioned. Observing it unilaterally, the grades are perfect for Momentum, Dividends, and Liquidity, with only Risk being subaverage- D for its high volatility and tracking error.

When it comes to price performance, the fund has etched out the best or comparative returns in the short term as well as the long term. Only SLVP has better performance on a longer time scale. Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL) is lagging behind, while Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR) is a relatively newer fund.

Short Term Catalysts, Structural Drivers Remain Strong

The near-term outlook for SILJ has suddenly and significantly changed with the outbreak of active U.S.-Iran military hostilities, which have now been superimposed upon an already powerful setup that was building before a single missile was launched. The coordinated U.S. and Israeli strikes against Iranian targets on February 28, 2026, saw silver react with a historic safe-haven move. Silver responded as it normally does with higher beta characteristics, as it went up as high as $96.40 per ounce before falling as much as 7% intraday, as it does as both an investment and industrial metal, seeing the investment side react to geopolitical risk with a positive response and seeing the industrial side react to recession risk from an oil price shock.

The Strait of Hormuz is the single most important variable in the equation, and the inflationary impact of the sustained disruption will, in turn, positively impact the monetary safe-haven bid for silver while creating additional complexities for the Federal Reserve, which could be forced to hold rates steady if the tensions with Iran remain elevated, creating additional complexities in the macro environment, even as the tensions themselves will positively impact precious metals.

The silver market had been building towards a breakout even before the geopolitical shock. The Section 232 critical mineral trade action represents yet another event that could push silver prices higher, albeit probabilities have been significantly reduced with the Presidential Proclamation. Silver has been formally included on the US Critical Minerals List for the first time in November 2025, and on January 14, 2026, a Presidential Proclamation has been issued, which directs negotiations with trading partners on critical mineral supply with a deadline of 180 days, with the US facing a stark strategic challenge as its production only meets 15% of annual consumption.

The structural bull case for SILJ is based on the increasing role of silver in the global energy transition and technology build-out. The Silver Institute estimates that there will be a sixth consecutive year of supply deficit in 2026, totaling 67 million ounces, and the market will continue to draw down above-ground inventory rather than increasing supply. This is because the supply deficit is not something that can be easily addressed; 75% of silver is mined as a byproduct of other metals, and even at record high silver prices, producers cannot increase supply independently of base metals.

On the demand side, silver’s enhanced electrical and thermal conductivity characteristics are becoming ever more vital to the technological revolution that is powering the global economy, with industrial demand poised to pick up significantly throughout 2030. Perhaps the most tangible structural story is in the automotive space, where global automotive demand for silver is forecast to grow at a 3.4% compound annual growth rate from 2025 to 2031, reaching 94 million ounces, with electric vehicles poised to surpass internal combustion engines as the dominant driver of automotive silver demand in 2027 and account for 59% of the total automotive demand in 2031 due to their significantly higher silver content in battery management systems and power electronic components.

Collectively, these are not cyclical buying trends; they’re permanent infrastructure requirements and represent the structural demand case for silver and, in turn, the junior miners represented in SILJ.

With regard to junior miners, the leverage is substantial over the longer term as prices increase and remain at higher levels, with junior exploration and production-stage companies seeing their valuation re-rate substantially as projects move beyond economic viability thresholds, thereby giving SILJ its high beta profile as a play on this multi-year bull market.

Valuation

Fund valuation doesn't look compelling compared to peer average, especially given last year’s rally. The fund P/E is 17.95x and is higher than the category average of Precious Metals as per Morningstar. On the other hand, the fund trades at a discount to the broader global materials theme, trading at around 24.75x P/E. Going over to the P/B ratio, it is somewhat uplifted, especially relative to the global materials companies, which are trading at around 2.65x P/B.

Historical cash flow growth and long term projected earnings are looking more than strong, with an average cash flow growth of around 54% for the past four years and long term earnings growth projections (three- to five year EPS growth) of around 22%.

From a technical perspective, the price is somewhat below the 20-Day MA, while the 20-Day MA is above the 50- and 200-Day MA, implying a golden cross pattern and suggesting an uptrend.

Risks

Investors need to have the entire risk profile with the same degree of clarity as the bull case. On March 3rd, with energy prices soaring due to the worsening Middle East war, silver prices declined sharply due to traders’ efforts to liquidate stocks, bonds, and other industrial commodities in response to rising interest rates and a rising US Dollar. This is the basic problem with silver in an inflationary war: with energy prices rising due to the war, inflation expectations increase, which in turn forces the Fed to become hawkish, which in turn forces the dollar to rise, which in turn forces silver prices to fall.

Apart from the geopolitical risks, the risks associated with the funds are those of any junior miner ETF. The fund is not diversified, and this could lead to higher volatility because of the volatility of individual issuers. The industrial need for silver could decrease if companies discover cheaper alternatives or reduce their silver usage due to price increases, and the need for silver could decrease from investors in response to macroeconomic changes, including higher interest rates or decreased inflation, making metals less attractive since they do not pay interest.

Conclusion

SILJ enters March 2026 at one of the most genuinely complex inflection points in its history, simultaneously possessing one of the most powerful structural commodity theses in decades and yet also being forced to contend with the acute volatility of an active U.S.-Iran military conflict whose duration and ultimate path remain deeply uncertain. The long-term fundamental argument, with six consecutive supply deficits, irreversible industrial demand trends in solar, EVs, and AI infrastructure, critical mineral designation with tariff implications, and a domestic production policy tailwind, has arguably never been stronger and has been further reinforced by the geopolitical environment. The near-term environment, on the other hand, remains substantially more treacherous and dependent on the impact of geopolitical turmoil on industrial demand and the macroeconomic environment. Tariff resolutions remain a significant theme, playing a key role in silver's trajectory. Therefore, I rate SILJ as a BUY while being vigilant of high volatility in the period ahead.

data.title: GE Vernova: Road To $1,000 As Estimate Revisions Keep Climbing
data.description:
Earlier this year, I released an article on GE Vernova (GEV) titled: "GE Vernova: The AI Power Bottleneck Trade Is Far From Over."

Well, as proven by the January guidance, which validated the continuation of the upward estimate revision cycle of the company, I think the power bottleneck trade is still far from its end.

In December 2025, management raised guidance, doubled the dividend, and lifted its buyback authorization to $10 billion. In January 2026, the company raised its 2026 revenue outlook to $44-$45 billion, above the $41.97 billion analyst consensus, triggering a fresh round of upward estimate revisions. In fact, as I'm about to show in this piece, some bears turned bulls after the strong Q4 results and guidance.

The main offset is still the Wind segment, which is still expected to lose about $400 million in EBITDA in 2026. Valuation is also rich, trading at 60x forward earnings. That said, I am not overly concerned about this multiple as long as hyperscaler capex and power infrastructure bottlenecks persist. Overall, I reiterate my strong buy rating, with a price target in mind of $1,000 before year-end.

The Estimate Upward Revision Cycle May Still Have Room To Run

It was back in December 2025 that GE Vernova updated investors with stronger guidance. I include the details below from the PR:

On top of that, the company doubled its dividend and increased the buyback authorization to $10 billion from $6 billion. I discussed this upgrade in my previous coverage, where I outlined why the AI power bottleneck trade is far from over.

In January, the company didn't disappoint investors after raising its 2026 and 2028 guidance. I include the full details below from the Q4 2025 earnings release:

To put those figures into perspective, consider that the new 2026 revenue guide ($44-$45B) was above the $41.97 billion analyst consensus compiled by LSEG. Therefore, I was not surprised in the slightest to see the Street moving up its revenue projections for the next four quarters after the Q4 earnings call.

The upgrade was significant enough to shake some of the bears. For example, Redburn double upgraded GE Vernova to a Buy (from Sell) and raised its target to $1,100, citing stronger than expected demand for power generation equipment.

Bear in mind that when they downgraded the stock to a sell in October, the downgrade was described as being driven by implausible long-term margin expectations, partly driven by the Wind segment. (I discuss the performance of this segment in the next section).

Moving on, the outlook for the balance sheet is also impressive. Even after issuing the debt for the Prolec acquisition ($5.275 billion), the company still expects to remain below 1x gross debt/adjusted EBITDA.

Speaking of Prolec, I think the acquisition was the right decision at the right time. In my view, the ownership of Prolec expands GE Vernova’s North American transformer footprint at the exact time the market is short on equipment.

As you may know, electrification gives GE Vernova exposure to the fastest-growing bottlenecks in the grid: transformers, switchgear, grid-stability equipment, and HVDC transmission. In 2025, Electrification orders increased to $19.3 billion, up 21% organically, and revenue rose to $9.6 billion, up 26% organically. The shortage is accretive to the bottom line as well, with this segment's EBITDA margin improving by 590 basis points.

The broader Electrification equipment backlog increased to $30.5 billion, up $10.5 billion, or 53% yoy. Interestingly enough, the growth seems to be broad-based and not only concentrated in the Americas:

From a 2026 guidance perspective, management now calls for Electrification revenue of $13.5 billion to $14.0 billion, including about $3 billion from Prolec, while maintaining 17%-19% for the segment EBITDA margin.

Analysts are slightly more optimistic about the outlook of this segment.

For example, S&P Global’s February report expects Electrification revenue to rise 44% to about $13.9 billion in 2026 and segment backlog to rise 31% to about $45.3 billion.

From a capacity perspective, back in December 2025, the CEO said that the company was sold out through 2028 and had less than 10% of its 2029 gas-turbine availability remaining.

More recently, some sources are suggesting that GE Vernova’s 2029 and 2030 capacity was nearly sold out and highlighted deals where customers were putting down nonrefundable deposits today for future deliveries.

Looking at the concrete numbers, the company expects gas-turbine contracts and slot reservations to reach about 100 GW in 2026, and CEO Scott Strazik said in a recent Bloomberg interview that he expects the gas turbine production capacity to keep up with rising global demand while total backlog reaches $200 billion by 2028.

Risks

The biggest risk that I see is the headwind (pun intended) coming from the Wind segment.

In the last earnings, management’s latest outlook still calls for Wind revenue to be down low-double digits in 2026. For reference, take a look at the revenue contraction of this segment in the last few quarters.

On top of that, management calls for about $400 million of Wind EBITDA losses in FY26, and Q1 2026 alone is guided to a $300 million-$400 million EBITDA loss.

In fact, if we look at the FY25 results, Wind losses were about $600 million. In December, management had guided to roughly $400 million of Wind EBITDA losses for 2025. For 2026, management now expects Wind EBITDA losses of about $400 million.

The rationale behind the deterioration seems to be the December 22, 2025, U.S. stop-work order on offshore wind projects. According to the company, that order directly affected the Vineyard Wind completion timeline. In fact, the company said that if the remaining 11 Vineyard turbines (from a total of 62) could not be installed, 2026 Wind revenue could be hit by about $250 million.

So what?
